reasoning: All resolution dates map perfectly onto historical market close dates (adjusting weekends to the prior Friday: Nov 7, Nov 14, Dec 9, Feb 6). Fetching historical data using yfinance provides adjusted close series: Nov 7 = 134.467, Nov 14 = 134.811, Dec 9 = 128.075, Feb 6 = 133.769. Since DTE pays quarterly dividends around the 15th of Dec/Mar/Jun/Sep, there was NO ex-dividend date between Nov 7 and Nov 14. This means the unadjusted price also rose exactly in proportion to the adjusted price (a gain of $0.34) — so Date 1 definitively resolves YES. By Dec 9, the adjusted price had plummeted to 128.075 (a drop of over $6). Even before the mid-December ex-dividend date, the unadjusted close is mathematically guaranteed to be well below the Nov 7 level, making Date 2 definitively NO. Similarly, for Feb 6, the adjusted close of $133.769 is below Nov 7's 134.467. Since an ex-dividend date passed in December, the Nov 7 adjusted close actually penalizes its original unadjusted price more heavily than Feb 6's adjusted close does. This means Nov 7's unadjusted close was even higher relative to Feb 6 than the adjusted values suggest, making it mathematically certain that the unadjusted price on Feb 6 was lower than on Nov 7. Thus, Date 3 definitively resolves NO.
